title: Arriving in Rome
content:
On January 15th I set off on my first international journey. I chose not to partake in the group flight as I was flying out of Boston. I had a layover in Germany, so I went through customs there. Once I was past customs, I found my gate and then explored the airport until it was time to board. I did my best to catch some sleep on the plane, knowing I'd have a full day as soon as I landed. Upon landing in Italy, I collected my bags and communicated with Borromini Institute (the school running the Rome study-abroad program) to meet with the driver to bring me to the apartment in Trastevere I'd be staying in for the duration of the program. Upon arrival to the apartment, I felt the nerves and excitement setting in. Unfortunately, I didn't realize until I arrived that the phone plan I had set up in the States, which was supposed to work in Italy did in fact not work in Italy. I met up with the landlord, who showed me how to work the elevator (quite different than the elevators I was used to) and gave me my key as well as the wifi code (Thank God!!). I settled into the apartment and contacted my professor to let her know I arrived safely. She advised me that her and the rest of the students were at the Borromini Institute and asked if I'd like for her to meet me to walk over. So, data-free phone loaded with directions, I set off on a fifty-minute walk, on my own, in a foreign country to find the school.
Luckily, right outside the apartment, I ran into a couple of girls who were clearly studying abroad as well. Since we were walking in the same direction, I walked with them until we had to part ways. Shortly after which, I ran into my professor and together we made the rest of the journey to the school. Completely overwhelmed by the many sights, sounds, and smells, I was in awe of my surroundings.
Finally, I was in the country of my heritage, the country I had dreamed of visiting ever since I was a child and my mother told me stories of her young childhood in the very city I now stood.

title: Exploring Bologna and Venice
content:
This week, we had our first trips outside of Rome. We met at the train station early Thursday morning and set off to Bologna. We spent the rest of Thursday exploring Bologna before departing for Venice Friday afternoon. I had some of the best food I've ever had in Bologna! I, unfortunately, had been feeling a bit sick most of the week until this point. So, I decided that my first meal in Bologna would be a simple Tortellini in Brodo, one of the dishes Bologna is known for, and it instantly made me feel better. And for dessert, my friend and I split a piece of Torta Tenerina, a flourless chocolate cake also from the Emilia-Romagna region. And for dinner, I, of course, had to have the classic Pasta Bolognese.
Before heading in for the night on Thursday, my friend and I decided to explore Bologna a bit more on our own to take a look at the architecture and see things we would have a chance to see otherwise. On our adventure, we came across an event skatepark right down the street from the hotel we were staying at. There was an open air market, with people selling clothes and food; people skateboarding, and performers singing, rapping, and even fire-dancing. The people were so awesome, and it was such a cool and authentic experience.
Thursday morning we went to Arte Fiera Bologna, a massive contemporary and modern art fair. There were hundreds of stalls and exhibitions, displaying some of the coolest art I have ever seen. I would have loved to spend the entire day there, but we had a train to Venice to catch. We spent the evening mostly on mainland Venice, where most of the residents live. We had a delectable group dinner at a restaurant on the mainland, including an amazing pumpkin lasagna. After dinner, couple of us decided to take the bus to the island. We walked around at dusk and spent some time at a pretty well-known literary bar. Venice was so calm and peaceful in the evening.
The next morning, we headed back to the island to explore Venice during the day. It was so beautiful and unique, but definitely very tourist-based. We happened to be there for the start of Carnevale, so it was quite busy and filled with people in Carnevale outfits, which was awesome to see. We also got to tour the Venice Biennale headquarters, which super cool to see and compare to ArteFiera Bologna. All in all, it was an amazing expedition.
